title: Development and validation of a multi-locus DNA metabarcoding method to identify endangered species in complex samples_SOP
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"cmrfk","text":"Background: DNA metabarcoding provides great potential for species identification in complex samples such as food supplements and traditional medicines. Such a method would aid CITES (the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora) enforcement officers to combat wildlife crime by preventing illegal trade of endangered plant and animal species. The objective of this research was to develop a multi-locus DNA metabarcoding method for forensic wildlife species identification and to evaluate the applicability and reproducibility of this approach across different laboratories.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8pf21","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b234m","text":"Results: A DNA metabarcoding method was developed that makes use of 12 DNA barcode markers that have demonstrated universal applicability across a wide range of plant and animal taxa, and that facilitate the identification of species in samples containing degraded DNA. The DNA metabarcoding method was developed based on Illumina MiSeq amplicon sequencing of well-defined experimental mixtures, for which a bioinformatics pipeline with user-friendly web interface was developed. The performance of the DNA metabarcoding method was assessed in an international validation trial by 16 laboratories, in which the method was found to be highly reproducible and sensitive enough to identify species present in a mixture at 1% dry weight content.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9bt20","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fjg8l","text":"Conclusion: The advanced multi-locus DNA metabarcoding method assessed in this study provides reliable and detailed data on the composition of complex food products, including information on the presence of CITES-listed species. The method can provide improved resolution for species identification, while verifying species with multiple DNA barcodes contributes to an enhanced quality assurance.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"7gq0l","text":"This DNA metabarcoding method has been designed for the qualitative identification of multiple species in complex samples. DNA metabarcoding combines DNA barcoding with next-generation sequencing (NGS), and makes use of universal PCR primers to mass-amplify and sequence one or more taxonomically informative DNA barcodes. In this SOP, the following animal DNA barcodes are used: COI-2, cytochrome b (cyt b), 16S ribosomal RNA (16S). The following plant DNA barcodes are used: maturase K (matK), ribulose 1-5 biphosphate carboxylase large subunit (rbcL), the chloroplast trnL (UAA) intron, and the Internal Transcribed Spacer 2 (ITS2) region. Full-length barcodes and mini-barcodes with reduced sequence lengths are used for 16S, COI-2, cyt b, rbcL, ITS2 and trnL to accommodate the identification of species in degraded DNA samples. All amplicons are amplified in singleplex PCR.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":405,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":489,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":548,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":571,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":741,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":744,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":759,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1iu0u","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aiuv3","text":"Samples ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ab9go","text":"The participating laboratories are provided with ten microcentrifuge tubes each containing 250 mg of powdered tissue stored in a cool box containing dry ice. The samples are labeled sample S1 to S10. In addition, two tubes containing positive control DNA (Bos taurus and Lactuca sativa) are shipped. The positive controls are labeled as 150 μl of 10 ng/μl Bos taurus and 150 μl of 10 ng/μl Lactuca sativa. ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":256,"length":11},{"style":"italic","offset":271,"length":14},{"style":"italic","offset":390,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8nfa4","text":"All samples should be stored frozen at -20°C until processed. Samples can be stored frozen indefinitely.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dap86","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":10},{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6kidg","text":"The raw Note: a user-friendly and platform-independent interface to the CITESspeciesDetect pipeline is currently under development, and is currently not available to the end-user.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":190,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"amnhh","text":"RIKILT will send the bioinformatics output to each participating partner for interpretation of results. The output will consist of the following files:","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"52t3u","text":"The BLAST results file containing the 20 best BLAST results (i.e. bit-score, e-value, accession numbers etc.) for each Operational Taxonomic Unit [OTU]. Note that the results of the CytB and CytB mini-barcodes have, for technical reasons, been combined and presented both under “CytB”. Also, the results of the COI-2 and COI-mini barcodes have been combined and presented both under “COI-2”.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"basp8","text":"The BLAST output file is a tab-separated file that can be opened using spreadsheet software (e.g. Excel) or text editor software (e.g. notepad++ available from https://notepad-plus-plus.org/). The file contains the BLAST results for the 12 barcode markers separated by brackets. For each DNA barcode marker the results file contains the following headers:","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"co7qo","text":"— qseqid OTU (query) sequence identifier with the associated number of NGS reads supporting the size of the OTU.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bq3k5","text":"— qlen OTU (query) sequence length.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"42v8s","text":"— qcovs Percentage OTU (query) coverage.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b0a6d","text":"— pident Percentage of identical matches.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9u75d","text":"— bitscore Bit score.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ekm57","text":"— evalue Expect value.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4bgn9","text":"— sacc Subject accession.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dhcho","text":"— staxids Unique subject taxonomy identifier.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ar6sd","text":"— species Subject scientific name.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dil0","text":"— synonym Subject synonym name.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fqiha","text":"— genus Subject genus name","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"42cqd","text":"— family Subject family name.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7qu3a","text":"— order Subject order name.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"c9fbe","text":"— Listed under CITES taxonomic listing (species/family/order).","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9b676","text":"— Category CITES appendix (I/II/III).","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9cgo4","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1d05e","text":"The BLAST tables are sorted based on bit score (bitscore) and it lists the most similar subject sequence(s) (sacc) on top. The BLAST output file often contains the concatenated hits for multiple OTUs (qseqid). For each DNA barcode marker these are named for example OTU_1;size=3863, OTU_2;size=1500, indicating that BLAST hits were found for two OTUs (OTU_1 and OTU_2), each of which are used in species identification. The size indicates the number of NGS reads that support that OTU cluster.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cf017","text":"Interpret the BLAST output and fill in tables 6 and 7 (Annex 1) using the following guidelines:","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e10i5","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2d5l9","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e72lb","text":"To minimize the chance of erroneous species identifications, it should be verified that at least three hits with highest (near identical) bit scores have the same species designation.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1dmdq","text":"If multiple (three or more) hits are obtained with highest bitscores, but with different assigned species, the OTU fragment is assumed to lack the discriminatory power at the species level. In such cases, the OTU would be identified at the genus-level.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3pqun","text":"If multiple (three or more) hits are obtained with highest bitscores, but with different assigned genera, the OTU fragment lacks the discriminatory power at the genus level. In such cases, the OTU would be identified at the family-level.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1ln2p","text":"If multiple (three or more) hits are obtained with highest bitscores, but with different assigned families, the OTU fragment lacks the discriminatory power at the family level. In such cases, the OTU would be identified at the order-level.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dubpi","text":"In case less than three hits (top hits and other) are reported, the OTU is identified at the family-level.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"74bb2","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1n4km","text":"Considerations when interpreting the (endangered) species identification: ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":74}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6d5fd","text":"The completeness of the reference database is of importance. The current underrepresentation of DNA barcodes from species protected under CITES may critically hamper their identification. The identification of CITES species (and other species) will improve as DNA barcoding campaigns continue, such as the Barcode of Wildlife Project (www.barcodeofwildlife.org).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"iee0","text":"The NCBI GenBank is known to contain erroneous taxonomic names and identifications. While it remains difficult to specify clear guidelines on how to recognise them, we recommend not to consider taxa with clearly deviant family or order assignments in BLAST results with otherwise unambiguous family listings. Also, GenBank entries without informative taxonomic information, such as scientific names containing “environmental sample”, should be ignored.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"32m84","text":"The CITESspeciesDetect pipeline uses a 98% sequence similarity threshold to distinguish species based on DNA barcodes. This cut-off was chosen based on studies that found this divergence threshold to be sufficient to keep the majority of plants and animals apart using the standard matK, rbcL, cytB and COI DNA barcoding markers (add CITATION). The plant mini-barcode markers of rbcL, trnL (P6 loop) and ITS2 do not provide species-level resolution at the 98% level in all cases, but may nonetheless provide valuable taxonomic information at the genus-or higher level in degraded DNA samples.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":282,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":288,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":303,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":379,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":385,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"camdm","text":"The OTU abundance as indicated by the size in the OTU query sequence identifier provides information about the number of Illumina reads supporting the OTU. Higher relative read numbers provide better support for the presence of an OTU compared to OTUs with lower read numbers. The OTU abundance should, however, not be interpreted in a quantitative manner (i.e. as a measure of species abundance).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ecdr2","text":"The quality of the identification depends on the length of the DNA barcode sequence used for identification. Smaller fragments have shown to lack the discriminatory power to distinguish between species in a genus or higher taxon. For this reason, the pipeline automatically discards identifications obtained from OTUs shorter than 200 nt, except for the ITS2, rbcL, and trnL (P6 loop) mini-barcode markers for which OTUs need to be longer than 150 nt, 140 nt and 10 nt, respectively.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":360,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":370,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ae3v3","text":"Species in diverse groups such as Cycadaceae, Orchidaceae, Cactaceae, Euphorbia sp., and primates can have very similar DNA barcode sequences. For this reason, it is often an entire genus or family that is listed by CITES, rather than individual plant or animal species. The presence of CITES-listed species can be confirmed when three or more top BLAST hits (see the above guidelines) are reported with taxa listed by CITES at the species or higher level (genus/family/order).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1p8o3","text":"CITES appendices contain multiple exceptions for certain taxa, e.g. based on their geographic location, domestication status or the enforcement of trade quota. The pipeline is unable to take these exceptions into account as they are not made available in a structured format. Therefore, the user is advised to revert to the speciesplus.net website for additional information on these details.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"do6ov","text":"","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"16h16","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"2s1g8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"590hq","text":" ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"omug","text":"References ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cldkt","text":"Chen S, Yao H, Han J, Liu C, Song J, Shi L, Zhu Y, Ma X, Gao T, Pang X (2010) Validation of the ITS2 region as a novel DNA barcode for identifying medicinal plant species. PloS one 5 (1):e8613","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dua0","text":"Fazekas AJ, Kuzmina ML, Newmaster SG, Hollingsworth PM (2012) DNA barcoding methods for land plants. Methods in molecular biology 858:223-252","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d0nas","text":"Geller J, Meyer C, Parker M, Hawk H (2013) Redesign of PCR primers for mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase subunit I for marine invertebrates and application in all-taxa biotic surveys. Nucleic Acids Research 8(19):4321-4326","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7qkbq","text":"Palumbi S. R. before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"bc73f","text":"Prepare the following solutions for CTAB DNA isolation:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":55}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1j870","text":"50 ml Chloroform","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9hpce","text":"20 ml Ice-cold ethanol (96%) (e.g. stored at -20ºC)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2bem9","text":"20 ml 70% ethanol","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"be0ep","text":"To prevent contamination: ","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":26}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5t7ra","text":"When performing the DNA isolation, make sure to have enough space between each tube.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d5imo","text":"When adding the DNA to the PCR, make sure that all the PCR tube caps are closed except the one that is used at that moment.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eim9g","text":"When pooling the samples for NGS analysis make sure that only the PCR tubes corresponding to the sample is opened.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Perform the DNA isolation for all the 11 samples simultaneously, including the DNA isolation of the negative control.
Weigh 100 +/-10 mg per sample in a 2 ml tube. undetermined, Sample
Add 300 μl nuclease-free molecular grade water. undetermined, Nuclease-free molecular grade water
Add 700 μl CTAB extraction buffer and vortex at maximum speed (~1000 rpm) for 10 seconds. undetermined, CTAB extraction buffer 00:00:10, Vortex
Add 5 μl RNase (100 mg/μl) solution and gently shake the tube. undetermined, RNase (100 mg/μl) solution
Incubate at 65 °C for 15 minutes in order to achieve efficient cell disruption in a Thermo-Shaker at maximum speed (~1000 rpm). 00:15:00, Incubation
Add 20 μl of proteinase K solution (20 mg/ml) and shake the vial again. undetermined, Proteinase K solution
Subsequently incubate at 65 °C for 1 h, Thermo-Shaker (1000 rpm). 01:00:00, Incubation
Centrifuge the sample at ~18,000 x g for 10 minutes. 00:10:00, Centrifugation
Fill a 1.5 ml Eppendorf tube with 500 μl of chloroform undetermined, Chloroform
Transfer the supernatant to the previously prepared 1.5 ml Eppendorf tube containing 500 μl of chloroform and shake the vial by hand for 30 seconds. 00:00:30, Shaking
Centrifuge at ~18,000 x g for 10 min. The two phases are clearly separated. 00:10:00, Centrifugation

Transfer upper layer to a new 1.5 ml tube (~700 μl) and add the equal volume of (700 μl) chloroform and shake for 30 seconds. 00:00:30, Shaking undetermined, Chloroform The supernatant to be transferred shall be transparent, and any elements of the blurry interface will result in unsuccessful DNA extraction and/or strong PCR inhibition later on.
Centrifuge for 10 minutes at ~18,000 x g. 00:10:00, Centrifugation
Transfer the upper layer to a new 2 ml tube. Based on previous experience ~500-600 μl (depending upon the sample) of upper layer can be transferred to the new tube.
Add 2 volumes of CTAB precipitation buffer and mix by pipetting up and down. To keep it even add 1200 μl of CTAB precipitation buffer for all the samples.
Incubate at room temperature for 1 h. 01:00:00, Incubation
Centrifuge the sample for 10 min at ~18,000 x g and remove the supernatant, 00:10:00, Centrifugation
Add 350 μl of NaCl (1.2 M) to the precipitate DNA (pellet not visible sometime). undetermined, NaCl
Add 350 μl of chloroform to the tube containing precipitated DNA and NaCl, and vortex for 30 seconds. undetermined, Chloroform 00:00:30, Vortex
Centrifuge the sample for 10 min at ~18,000 x g 00:10:00, Centrifugation
Transfer the upper layer to a new 1.5 ml tube.
Add 2 volumes of ice-cold ethanol (96%) mix the sample by gently inverting the tube several times. Here, 700 μl of ice-cold ethanol needs to be added, to keep it even for all the samples.
The samples are now stored overnight at -20 °C.
On another day Centrifuge for 30 minutes at ~18,000 x g and remove the supernatant. 00:30:00, Centrifugation
Add 500 μl of ethanol (70%) to wash the pellet. undetermined, Ethanol (70%)

Centrifuge for 5 minutes at ~18,000 x g and remove the supernatant by pipetting. 00:05:00, Centrifugation Make sure that the supernatant is completely removed and also make sure not to displace the pellet.
Air-dry the pellet for 1 hr. 01:00:00, Air-drying
Add 100 μl of water and let the pellet dissolve overnight at 4 °C. undetermined, water
On another day Vortex the dissolved DNA and short-spin (10 sec) using mini-centrifuge. 00:00:10, Centrifuge

The concentration of dissolved DNA is measured using by spectrophotometry (e.g. NanoDrop ND1000) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. No DNA yield should be present in the DNA isolation negative control.
100 μl of DNA is required for the experiment, dilute the DNA to a final concentration of 10 ng/μl using nuclease-free molecular grade water.
Label (sample name, date) the 1.5 ml tubes containing the purified DNA and store the DNA at 4 °C.

Prepare for each DNA barcode marker primer pair (see the table below for primer information) a PCR mixture (12 in total) in a 1.5 ml Eppendorf tube using the provided chemicals and volumes listed under “Volumes (μl) X 13)” (Table 1). Primer labelling information: %s %s Before making the 12 master mixes, thaw the primers, vortex and spin down using mini-centrifuge. For example, use the primers 16SF and 16SR for PCR amplification of the full-length 16S marker. a

Vortex the PCR mixture for 10 sec. Vortexing will cause the liquid to be trapped in the cap of the tube. Before proceeding to the next step, make sure that this liquid is returned to the rest of the mixture by a15-second spin-down in a mini-centrifuge.
Aliquot 20 μl of PCR mixture to each well according to the scheme in Table 3. undetermined, PCR mixture
Aliquot 5 μl of sample DNA template (10 ng/μl) to each well according to the pipetting scheme in Table 3. Add 5 μl of the positive control DNA according to the setup in Table 3.

Use the provided Bos taurus DNA (10 ng/μl) as positive control DNA for the animal barcode markers. Use the provided Lactuca sativa DNA (10 ng/μl) as positive control DNA for the plant barcode markers. Add 5 μl of ddH2O instead of DNA as negative control according to the setup in Table 3.

Place the PCR tube strips in a thermocycler block with plastic caps on top and close the lid of the thermocycler. The second prepared example plate 2 can be stored at 4°C until further use in case only one PCR machine is available.
Start the PCR program with the following extended thermal profile: a
Examine the PCR thermocycler to ensure that the run has completed at the correct temperatures and that there is no error messages.

S – Sample, PA- Positive control animal, PP- Positive control plant and NC- Negative control after the PCR run has been successfully completed, spin down all the PCR tube strips for 15 seconds using a mini-centrifuge to make sure that any liquid that might be trapped under the cap is returned to the rest of the cocktail.
Gently remove the cap of the PCR tube strip.

Visualise 5 μl of the sample S1-S10, positive control and negative control reactions by 1% agarose gel electrophoresis using an appropriate staining method (e.g. ethidium bromide) or on a microchip electrophoresis system. The fragment of the amplicons in the samples and the positive control reaction is verified by comparison to a 100 bp DNA Ladder. The expected fragment sizes of the amplicons are specified in the table below. a
An example gel image is included in Figure 1. No DNA bands should be observed for the negative control reactions.
The amplified products in the PCR tubes can be stored at 4 °C before pooling.
On another day Vortex all the PCR tubes for 5 sec and spin-down using a micro-centrifuge for 10 sec. 00:00:05, Vortex 00:00:10, Spin-down
Transfer 8 μl of each PCR reaction mixture of sample S1 to a single 1.5 ml Eppendorf tube (row A, from A1 to A12, of Plate 1). In this way, all different barcoding markers of sample S1 are pooled.
Repeat this procedure for samples S2 to S10.

The pooled PCR products of samples S1 to S10 are purified using the QIAquick PCR purification kit according to the manufacturer’s protocol QIAGEN (Annex 3). Ethanol is already added to the PE buffer, ready to use.
Columns are eluted with 30 μl of the Elution buffer provided by the manufacturer. undetermined, Elution buffer

Quantify the pooled and purified PCR products by spectrophotometry (e.g. Nanodrop ND1000) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. If the concentration of the pooled and purified PCR products is less than 15 ng/μl, then repeat the QIAquick PCR purification step for these samples using higher volumes (> 10 μl) of the PCR reaction mixtures.
For each sample, prepare an aliquot of at least 12.5 μl and with a concentration of at least 15 ng/μl.
Label (sample name, date, and laboratory-number) the purified PCR products and store the amplicons at 4 °C.
creator: Alfred J Arulandhu
